title: The Alfirin Tribes
excerpt: The **Alfirin** are the Immortal Ones, the first of the elder peoples to set foot upon the Lands of Legends.
content_markdown:
# The Alfirin Tribes
The **Alfirin** are the Immortal Ones, the first of the elder peoples to set foot upon the Lands of Legends.
# Origins of the Alfirin
In the deepest reaches of Alfirin memory there lies a time **before this world**, before forests took root and before the stars shone in their current courses. This age is known only as **The First Homeland**, a realm now lost to both space and time. Its true name has been erased from all but the most sacred songs, for to speak it is said to invite sorrow and remembrance too heavy for even immortal hearts.
The First Homeland was a world of harmony and radiant order, where the Alfirin lived in unity with forces that blurred the line between matter, magic, and thought. Cities of living light drifted above crystal seas, and the boundaries between realms were thin and gently navigated. It was there that the Alfirin first learned to sail not merely upon water, but upon **the Aether Between Worlds**.
---
## The Cataclysm of Unmaking
No single account agrees on what caused the fall of the First Homeland.
Some ancient Sky Children texts speak of **hubris**, claiming the Alfirin reached too far into the hidden structures of reality, attempting to bind eternity itself. Galadhrim oral traditions instead whisper of a **wound inflicted upon the world**, an imbalance that grew until the land could no longer sustain life. The Nightshades teach that the catastrophe was inevitable: a calculated convergence of cosmic forces that no wisdom could have prevented.
What all accounts agree upon is this: **the Cataclysm was sudden, total, and irreversible**.
The skies tore open. Seas boiled into light. Continents fractured into drifting shards of matter and memory. Entire cities were lost in moments, unmade not by fire, but by unraveling existence itself. As reality collapsed, the Alfirin faced extinction.
---
## The Great Exodus
In desperation, the greatest minds and artisans of the Alfirin enacted a plan conceived only as theory: the **Exodus Through the Dimensional Aether**.
Using vast aether-sails, star-forges, and engines bound with living runes, they transformed their greatest vessels into **World-Sailors**—ships capable of surviving outside conventional reality. These vessels did not travel _through_ space, but _between_ spaces, gliding along currents of fractured dimensions and half-formed worlds.
The journey was long beyond reckoning. Time behaved strangely within the Aether. Some ships vanished entirely. Others emerged changed, their crews altered in mind, form, or purpose. Legends tell of Alfirin who stepped beyond the ships and became something else entirely, dissolving into the endless between.
Those who survived called themselves **the Alfirin**, meaning _the Enduring Ones_—not because they had conquered fate, but because they had endured what should have erased them.
---
## Arrival in the Lands of Legends
At last, the surviving vessels were drawn toward a young and fertile world, rich with raw potential. Its dimensions were stable, its magic deep and unclaimed. Guided by fading star-maps and instinct older than memory, the World-Sailors descended upon the western coasts of this realm.
Their arrival did not pass unnoticed. The skies burned briefly with auroral fire, tides surged, and strange stars appeared for a single night before fading. Where the Alfirin made landfall, the foundations of what would become **[[Cird]]** were laid, its harbors shaped first not by hands, but by resonant aether-song.
To the Alfirin, this world was not a conquest, but a **refuge**—and perhaps a second chance.
---
## The First Sundering
Survival did not bring unity.
The trauma of the Cataclysm and the long passage through the Aether had changed the Alfirin. Old philosophies fractured under the weight of loss. Some believed the new world must be protected at all costs. Others believed it must be guided, shaped, and prepared. A few believed that isolation and secrecy were the only paths to true survival.
Thus came the **First Sundering**, when the Alfirin ceased to be one people in spirit, though they remained so in blood.
From this division arose the paths that would later be known as:
- **The Sky Children**, who embraced the open world and the younger races
- **The Tree People**, who bound themselves to the living land
- **The Children of the Deep,** who submerged themselves under water
- **The Nightshades**, who turned to shadow, secrecy, and long designs
Though centuries—perhaps millennia—passed before these identities fully crystallized, the seeds of all future conflict were planted in those earliest days upon foreign shores.
---
## Legacy of the Lost World
Even now, echoes of the First Homeland linger.
Fragments of impossible material sometimes fall from the sky. Ancient Alfirin relics hum softly when exposed to starlight. Certain dreams, shared across all three kindreds, depict the same ruined skyline beneath a shattered sky.
The Alfirin do not agree on whether their lost world can—or should—be found again.
But all know this truth:
They are a people born of **cataclysm, exile, and survival**, sailing not only across seas, but across realities themselves. And though they have made new homes, **they have never truly stopped being refugees of a dead world**.
---
## The Sky Children
_(Kalaquendi, Caledhel, Meneldil)_
The **Sky Children** are the most widely known and commonly encountered of the Alfirin, and to many mortals they are _the_ Alfirin. Calling themselves the **Meneldil**, or _Friends of Heaven_, they settled along the coasts and open plains near Cird, building soaring cities of pale stone, glass, and wind-sculpted spires.
The Sky Children are defined by curiosity and openness. They delight in travel, trade, diplomacy, and the exchange of ideas with other peoples. Unlike their kin, they believe that the destiny of the world must be shaped _together_ with mortals, not apart from them.
Physically, the Meneldil are unmistakable: tall and slender, with pointed ears and skin ranging from pale azure to deep cerulean, said to reflect the light of the sky under which they were born. Their eyes often gleam like stars or polished crystal.
Their society is a **highly refined meritocracy**, governed by a **High Council** composed of scholars, artisans, strategists, and visionaries. In theory, rank is earned through achievement and service. In practice, because the Sky Children are immortal, advancement can take centuries—and what an immortal considers _merit_ is often opaque and deeply philosophical. This has led to quiet resentment among younger members and subtle political maneuvering within the councils.
The Sky Children excel at:
- Diplomacy and long-term planning
- Sky- and light-aspected magic
- Navigation, astronomy, and shipbuilding
- Preservation of ancient lore
They are often seen as benevolent, aloof, and faintly melancholy, for they alone among the Alfirin openly mourn the passing of mortal friends.
---
## The Tree People
_(Galadhrim, Aldamir)_
The **Tree People**, known among themselves as the **Galadhrim**, broke away early from the Sky Children, journeying south into the vast primeval forests. Where their kin built cities, the Galadhrim chose roots, branches, and living stone. Over countless ages, they became something neither fully civilized nor truly feral—but utterly inseparable from the wild.
Modern Galadhrim live in **loose tribal confederations**, each bound to a specific forest, grove, or spirit-domain. Their settlements are grown rather than built: woven boughs, living platforms, and hollowed elder-trees that shift subtly with the seasons. To outsiders, their lands appear untamed and dangerous, yet every vine and beast obeys an ancient balance enforced by the Tree People.
The Galadhrim possess **innate nature magic**, not learned through study but expressed instinctively. This magic manifests as rapid plant growth, command over beasts, shifting terrain, and primal curses against those who despoil the wilds. They do not see this as sorcery but as _conversation_ with the world.
Physically, the Tree People are lithe and sinewy, with pointed ears, green-hued skin, and hair often streaked with bark-brown or leaf-gold tones. Many bear natural markings resembling vines, moss, or veins of wood.
They are seldom seen beyond their forests, and when they are, it is usually as:
- Silent wardens tracking a threat
- Envoys bearing warnings
- Reluctant allies against world-threatening dangers
To the Galadhrim, civilization is a temporary sickness, forests are eternal, and the Alfirin exist to **guard the living memory of the world**.
---
## The Children of the Deep
**Falthrim, Falmarin, The Tide-Born**
Long before the Alfirin cities took root upon land, a final splinter chose **not to remain upon the shore at all**.
These were the **Falthrim**—the Children of the Deep—who followed the great currents westward and downward, abandoning stone and sky alike. Where others built towers or forests, the Falthrim learned the slow language of pressure, darkness, and endless motion.
Their home is **Mar-nu-Falmar**, the World-Below-the-Waves, a shifting realm of coral palaces, kelp-cathedrals, and drifting sanctuaries carried by the tides.
To most Alfirin, the Falthrim are considered **lost**.
To the sea, they are still present.
---
### Ways of the Deep
The Falthrim live **nomadic lives bound to the great sea-beasts**, particularly the ancient whale-clans who remember songs older than continents. Entire Falthrim communities migrate with these leviathans, herding, guarding, and communing with them across vast oceanic routes.
They do not claim territory.
They follow memory.
Their society is fluid and cyclical, governed by Tide-Councils whose authority rises and falls like the seas themselves. Leadership is not permanent—those who guide during one migration may become listeners in the next.
---
### Culture and Belief
Falthrim philosophy teaches that **nothing truly endures unless it moves**.
They see surface dwellers as dangerously static, especially their Alfirin kin who cling to cities, immortality, and fixed form. To the Falthrim, the sea is the only honest teacher of eternity—relentless, patient, and ever-changing.
They venerate no gods in the traditional sense, but honor:
- The Great Currents
- The Deep Silence
- The Songs of the Leviathans
Among them, memory is sung rather than written, and history is carried through resonance rather than record.
---
### Appearance
Falthrim share the Alfirin’s immortal bearing but are shaped by the deep:
- Skin in shades of pearl, deep blue, sea-green, or abyssal black
- Eyes reflective and lightless, adapted to darkness
- Hair flowing like kelp or drifting silt
- Slender but powerful builds suited for pressure and long travel
They retain the characteristic pointed ears, though these are often finned or webbed.
---
### Relations with Other Alfirin
**Kalaquendi (Sky Children):** Viewed as naïve and overly visible
**Galadhrim (Tree People):** Respected for their harmony, but pitied for their rootedness
**Duathrim (Nightshades):** Watched warily; the deep remembers their quiet violence
The Falthrim rarely interfere in surface affairs. When they do, it is often mistaken for myth: ghost-lights beneath waves, ships guided from storms, or fleets vanishing into unnatural calms.
---
### Rumors and Truths
Whispered among sailors and Velim alike:
- The Falthrim still guard **sunken Gate-fragments**
- They can hear the **deep resonance of Daelgast** where it presses against the ocean floor
- The Leviathans may remember the world **before the Scar**
If true, the Falthrim may hold knowledge no other people possess—knowledge earned not through power, but through patience.
---
### A Saying of the Falthrim
> _“The shore remembers impact._
> _The sea remembers everything.”_

---
## The Nightshades
_(Mornedhel, Moredhel, Duath, Watharim, Duathrim)_
The **Nightshades**, who call themselves the **Duath** or **Duathrim**, are the most feared and least understood of the Alfirin. In the aftermath of the original schism, this smaller faction rejected both cooperation with mortals and communion with nature. Instead, they turned northward, beyond known maps and mortal reach.
For centuries they were believed lost—until whispered reports began to circulate of the **[[Nightshades]]**: flawless assassins who struck without warning, left no trace, and vanished into darkness. These rumors proved true. The Duathrim had survived, and thrived, in the frozen lands beyond **[[The Sands]]**, establishing their hidden dominion in **[[Lomêanor]]**, the _Land of Shadow_.
Duathrim society is a **strictly hierarchical, militarized order**. From early childhood, every individual is tested, trained, and sorted into a **guild**—arcane, political, martial, or covert. From there, they are assigned to autonomous three-person cells known as **Duathan**, each designed to operate independently for years or even decades.
Their culture blends **arcane mastery with disciplined martial tradition**. Magic is studied as a weapon, secrecy as a virtue, and patience as the greatest form of power. The ultimate goal of the Duathrim is nothing less than the silent domination of the world: to infiltrate all nations, manipulate history from the shadows, and in time **erase all peoples but the Alfirin**.
Physically, Nightshades possess pointed ears and skin tones ranging from ghostly white through ashen violet to absolute black. Among the Duathrim, skin tone is culturally significant:
- **Lighter skin** is associated with arcane sensitivity, strategy, and governance
- **Darker skin** signifies martial prowess, endurance, and talent for assassination
This belief is reinforced through training and tradition, shaping both caste and expectation.
To the world at large, the Nightshades are myth. To rulers and secret orders, they are a nightmare—proof that history may not be written by those who appear in it.
---
Together, the kindreds are known in ancient Alfirin lore as **the Fourfold People**, bound by origin, divided by purpose. Whether they will one day reunite—or destroy one another along with the younger races—remains one of the great unanswered questions of the Lands of Legends.
